frameworks.name: Reversibility Matrix
frameworks.description: Every commitment exists on a spectrum from fully reversible to permanently irreversible. Most decisions are more reversible than they feel. Treating reversible decisions as irreversible is the primary cause of decision paralysis and missed opportunity.
steps: Before any significant commitment, ask: what is the actual cost to reverse this decision in 6 months?
Classify: Type 1 (one-way door — truly irreversible or very high reversal cost) vs Type 2 (two-way door — reversible at manageable cost)
Estimate the reversal cost honestly: financial, relational, reputational, time
For Type 2 decisions: move fast, execute, observe, reverse if wrong — do not treat as Type 1
For Type 1 decisions: slow down, analyze deeply, consult broadly, decide carefully
Audit your recent decisions: which Type 2 decisions did you treat as Type 1? What did the delay cost?
antipatterns: Applying Type 1 analysis to Type 2 decisions — produces paralysis and missed opportunities
No reversal cost estimation — prevents accurate classification
Sunk cost fallacy: past investment making a Type 2 decision feel irreversible when it is not

frameworks.name: Optionality Preservation Protocol
frameworks.description: Optionality is the possession of future choices. It is depleted by commitments, specialization, and debt. It is expanded by skills, networks, financial reserves, and maintained relationships. Actively managed optionality is structural agency.
steps: Audit your current optionality: what options do you currently have that you did not have 2 years ago? What options have you lost?
Identify optionality drains: long-term commitments, financial obligations, and specialization that creates dependency
Identify optionality builders: skills, relationships, capital, reputation, geographic flexibility
For any major commitment, ask: does this expand or contract my future options? Is the gain worth the optionality cost?
Maintain warm relationships with alternatives: do not let exit options go cold before you need them
Reserve capital explicitly for optionality: an investment in an unknown future opportunity is the highest-returning asset class
antipatterns: Optimizing current position at the cost of all future flexibility — perfect execution of a wrong strategy
No maintained alternatives — options that go cold are not actually available when needed
Excessive specialization without adjacent skills — creates dependency, not leverage

frameworks.name: Anti-Lock-In Design
frameworks.description: Lock-in is the gradual reduction of options through accumulated dependencies. Most lock-in is not chosen — it accumulates through inattention. Anti-lock-in design makes dependency visible and deliberate.
steps: Map your current dependencies: who or what do you depend on that you could not easily replace?
Classify each dependency: chosen (worth the lock-in for the value received) vs. accidental (not consciously chosen, just accumulated)
For each accidental dependency: design the exit path now, before you need it
Vendor/platform lock-in: prefer open standards, portable data, and multi-vendor strategies
Income lock-in: single-employer or single-client dependency is the most common and costly lock-in pattern
Geographic lock-in: presence in a single market concentrates risk; remote capability preserves optionality
antipatterns: Dependencies that are invisible until they fail — map them proactively
Accepting lock-in for convenience — often the long-term cost far exceeds the short-term gain
No exit path design for current dependencies — optionality that requires months to build is not available in a crisis

prompts.name: Reversibility Matrix Audit
prompts.template: Audit my current commitments for reversibility. Major commitments (work, financial, relational, geographic): [list]. For each, classify as Type 1 (truly irreversible) or Type 2 (reversible at manageable cost) and estimate the reversal cost. Identify any Type 2 decisions I have been treating as Type 1.

prompts.name: Optionality Portfolio Audit
prompts.template: Audit my optionality portfolio. Options I have now that I did not have 2 years ago: [list]. Options I have lost in 2 years: [list]. Current optionality drains (commitments that limit future choices): [list]. Current optionality builders (skills, capital, relationships, flexibility): [list]. Identify my net optionality trajectory and the highest-leverage action to expand future options.

prompts.name: Lock-In Dependency Map
prompts.template: Map my current lock-in dependencies. Income dependencies: [describe]. Platform/vendor dependencies: [describe]. Geographic dependencies: [describe]. Relationship dependencies: [describe]. Classify each as chosen or accidental. For each accidental dependency, give me the exit path design.

examples.title: Consultant Eliminating Single-Client Dependency
examples.context: Independent consultant with 80% of revenue from one client. High income, low optionality.
examples.lock_in_type: Accidental income lock-in — evolved from convenience, not decision
examples.intervention: Mapped dependency explicitly. Set 12-month goal: no single client above 40% of revenue. Proactively developed 3 new client relationships from network.
examples.outcome: 18 months later: largest client = 35% revenue. Subsequent rate increase negotiation — previously avoided for fear of losing the client — resulted in 25% price increase accepted without friction.

vocabulary.definition: An irreversible or very high reversal cost commitment. Warrants deep analysis and deliberate process. Approximately 5% of decisions.
A reversible commitment where errors are correctable at manageable cost. Warrants fast execution and learning. Approximately 95% of decisions.
The possession of future choices. A structural asset that enables agency. Depleted by commitments; built by skills, capital, and relationships.
Accumulated dependencies that reduce future options. Often not consciously chosen — it accumulates through inattention to reversibility.
The actual financial, relational, reputational, and time cost of undoing a commitment. The key variable in reversibility classification.
Proactive mapping of dependencies and deliberate design of exit paths before they are needed. Converts accidental lock-in into chosen lock-in or eliminates it.

sources.title: Antifragile
sources.relevance: Optionality as asset class, barbell strategy, convexity

v78_consciousnesses: Ancient One v3.0 (temporal evaluation of option value)
master_laws: Law 3 (Agency — option preservation is the direct implementation of Law 3)
Law 30 (Exit — always have a way out)
sovereign_needs_served: Agency (primary — this cartridge is the direct implementation of Agency need)

4_gate_validation.high_leverage: PASS — optionality is a meta-asset multiplying all other strategic moves
